title: Adaptive Dominant Zone Selection in Commercial Buildings Using Machine Learning

slides.voice: In large commercial buildings, cooling isn't uniform. A phenomenon exists where a tiny fraction of rooms, called dominant zones, actually drive the energy load for the entire building. When one of these zones struggles to stay cool, it sends a cooling request to the central Air Handler. In response, the handler drops the supply air temperature for everyone. This effectively overcools the majority of the building just to satisfy the high demand of a few special rooms.
slides.goal: Understand the physical relationship between individual zone requests and building-wide HVAC behavior.
slides.remarks: Create a central rectangle representing an 'AHU' and five smaller rectangles surrounding it as 'Zones'. Animate a heat gradient (orange) growing in two zones while others remain green. Show dashed arrows moving from the orange zones to the AHU labeled 'Cooling Request'. As arrows hit the AHU, change its color from light blue to deep blue. Then, show deep blue 'Supply Air' arrows flowing back to ALL zones. The green zones should then turn a 'chilled' white-blue color to signify overcooling.

slides.voice: To predict which zones will be dominant tomorrow, we use a logistic regression model for every single zone. Instead of looking at raw spikes, we look at the 'memory' of the zone. We calculate two main features: the average temperature gap from the setpoint over the last 24 hours, and the average over the last 30 days. This allows the model to distinguish between a temporary door being left open and a zone that is fundamentally struggling with its cooling load.
slides.goal: Visualize the mathematical inputs and the logistic mapping used for prediction.
slides.remarks: Display a NumberPlane. On the right, show a sigmoid curve. Create two sliding bars on the left labeled '1-Day T-CSP' and '30-Day T-CSP'. As the bars slide up and down, animate a dot moving along the sigmoid curve. Use TransformMatchingTex to show how the feature vector X scales the weights in the logistic formula to move the probability P(y=1) between 0 and 1.

slides.voice: We have to decide on a probability threshold. If we set it too low, we flag way too many zones as dominant, which is inefficient. If we set it too high, we miss critical cooling requests. By plotting the recall—how many requests we correctly caught—against the fraction of zones flagged, we look for the 'elbow' of the curve. This is the sweet spot where we maximize our catch rate while keeping the number of targeted zones as small as possible.
slides.goal: Explain the trade-off between sensitivity and selectivity in model tuning.
slides.remarks: Create an Axes with x_axis='Fraction of Zones Flagged' and y_axis='Recall'. Plot a concave curve that rises sharply then levels off (representing the validation results). Use a ValueTracker to move a highlighted point along the curve. As the point moves left, use a side-panel to show a building floorplan where fewer 'Zone' squares are highlighted; as it moves right, more squares light up. Highlight the 'elbow' with a glowing circle.

slides.voice: Traditional industry standards use static 'rules of thumb' that never change. But dominance is dynamic. Our machine learning approach is adaptive; it can recognize when a zone stops being a problem due to cooler weather or shifts in occupancy. While the static benchmark stays stuck, the logistic model constantly updates its list, flagging more zones during heatwaves and fewer when the building is under less stress, ensuring high performance without wasting energy.
slides.goal: Demonstrate the superiority of adaptive ML over static ASHRAE guidelines.
slides.remarks: Create a split-screen animation. On the left, 'Static Benchmark'; on the right, 'Logistic Model'. Show a timeline moving across the bottom (May to September). On the left, the highlighted zones in a floorplan remain frozen. On the right, animate the highlighted zones flickering on and off and changing in count as the timeline progresses. Overlay a line graph showing 'Missed Requests' (Recall error) where the ML line stays significantly lower than the Static line.
